finding: The three hero proof-statistics render as literal zeros — '0+' entrepreneurs launched, '$0M+' sales, '0+' vending locations — so the site's headline social proof communicates that nothing has happened.

evidence.quote:
0+
ENTREPRENEURS LAUNCHED
$0M+
SNACK/DRINK SALES IN VENDINGPRENEUR MACHINES
0+
VENDING LOCATIONS W/OUR GUIDANCE
stat block shows 0+ and $0M+

persona_rationale: Proof numbers are the first thing I check before believing any program works. Stats that read as zeros are worse than no stats — they tell me either the program has zero results or the team ships broken pages. Either way I'm out.

suggested_fix.current: 0+ Entrepreneurs launched / $0M+ Snack-drink sales / 0+ Vending locations
suggested_fix.proposed: Populate real verified figures (e.g. '600+ entrepreneurs launched', '$12M+ in machine sales', '2,000+ locations'); if the count-up animation is failing, render the final value server-side as a fallback so it never shows 0

finding: No price, price range, or explanation of how pricing works appears anywhere on the public site; the program asks for an application with the cost entirely hidden.

evidence.quote: THE VENDING ACCELERATOR PROGRAM
All fees for courses, memberships, and digital products are stated at the time of purchase.

persona_rationale: Hidden pricing is my number-one walk-away trigger. If I have to book a call to find out what it costs, I assume the price is high enough that they don't want it compared. I won't apply blind.

suggested_fix.current: No pricing anywhere; cost revealed only after an application/call
suggested_fix.proposed: Add a Pricing or 'What it costs' section (a 'from $X' figure, a price band, or at minimum 'investment discussed on your call, typically $X-$Y range, no obligation')

finding: The hero and supporting copy lean on 'financial freedom' and 'passive income' with 'minimal time investment' — the exact framing of get-rich-quick funnels that a skeptical buyer is trained to distrust.

evidence.quote: TURN VENDING INTO YOUR PATH TO FINANCIAL FREEDOM
launch and scale a profitable vending machine business with minimal time investment.

persona_rationale: 'Financial freedom with minimal time investment' is the sentence every overselling course uses. It makes me assume the substance is thin and the marketing is doing the heavy lifting.

suggested_fix.current: Turn Vending Into Your Path to Financial Freedom … with minimal time investment
suggested_fix.proposed: Lead with a concrete, defensible promise (e.g. 'Learn the exact system to place your first profitable vending machine in 90 days') and drop 'minimal time investment'

finding: Testimonials carry no dates, several use first-name-plus-initial only, and none link to a verifiable profile — making the social proof unfalsifiable.

evidence.quote:
ADAM S

Owner, Emporio Vending LLC

5 out of 5 stars

ABBY C

Van Pelt Vending

5 out of 5 stars

persona_rationale: I can't verify a testimonial with no date and a half-name. Anonymous, undated praise is the cheapest thing to fabricate, so it counts for almost nothing in my evaluation.

suggested_fix.current: Name-only testimonials, no dates, no links (e.g. 'ADAM S')
suggested_fix.proposed: Add a month/year and, where possible, a linked LinkedIn/business profile or named full attribution to each testimonial

finding: A specific income figure ('making $10,000 a month in vending machines') is quoted approvingly inside a homepage testimonial with no earnings disclaimer anywhere near it.

evidence.quote: I've had an article about a successful entrepreneur (making $10,000 a month in vending machines) bookmarked on my phone

persona_rationale: Dangling a $10k/month number with no 'results not typical' caveat is exactly the FTC-risky overselling that's burned me before. It tells me the marketing outruns the substance, and it's a legal liability red flag.

suggested_fix.current: $10,000/month figure in a testimonial, no disclaimer nearby
suggested_fix.proposed: Add a visible earnings disclaimer adjacent to any income claim ('Individual results vary; this is not a guarantee of income') and link it to the Terms income-disclaimer section

finding: The homepage has a serious color-contrast violation flagged by axe.

evidence.quote: / … color-contrast | serious | Ensure the contrast between foreground and background colors meets WCAG 2 AA minimum

persona_rationale: A serious contrast failure on the front page is the kind of polish gap that tells me the team didn't QA carefully — and if they skipped that, what else did they skip on the product I'd be paying for?

suggested_fix.current: Foreground/background pair below WCAG AA 4.5:1 on the homepage
suggested_fix.proposed: Adjust the offending text/background colors to meet at least 4.5:1 contrast

finding: A 'Featured partners' logo wall (PepsiCo, Doritos, Poppi, Prime, Cantaloupe, 365 Retail Markets) implies major-brand endorsement without stating the actual relationship.

evidence.quote:
region "Featured partners":
    - img "365 Retail Markets" … "PepsiCo" … "Doritos" … "Poppi"

persona_rationale: Plastering Pepsi and Doritos logos without saying 'these are products we help you source' vs 'these companies endorse us' is borrowed credibility. As a marketer I know the trick, and it makes me trust the rest of the page less.

suggested_fix.proposed: Relabel honestly to the real relationship, e.g. 'Brands you can source through our supplier network' or 'Products our members stock'

finding: The founder is presented by first name only ('Mike') with no surname, credentials, company registration, or verifiable identity.

evidence.quote: MEET MIKE
img "Mike, founder of Vendingpreneurs"

persona_rationale: I'm being asked to trust this person with my money and my contact details, but I can't even Google him — there's no last name. Anonymity at the top of a paid program is a major credibility hole.

suggested_fix.proposed: Use his full name, a one-line verifiable bio, and link to a LinkedIn/business profile and the registered company entity (Modern Amenities) named in the legal pages

finding: An explicit 'I'm not here to sell a dream' line on the About page reads as protesting too much and raises rather than lowers suspicion.

evidence.quote: I'm not here to sell a dream, I'm here to show you a system that works.

persona_rationale: When a sales page tells me it isn't selling me anything, that's exactly when I tighten my grip on my wallet. Trust is shown through proof, not asserted in copy.

suggested_fix.current: I'm not here to sell a dream, I'm here to show you a system that works.
suggested_fix.proposed: Replace the assertion with evidence: a concrete, dated outcome ('Here's the 4-step system, and here are three members who used it in the last 12 months — see case studies')

finding: Specific personal financial figures are presented as proof of credibility with no substantiation ($1,200/mo job, $70k fixer-upper, $250k condo, 'thousands of dollars in monthly income').

evidence.quote: I was working a 60-hour per week job that paid me only $1,200/month. I used my emergency fund to invest 20% down on a $70,000 fixer upper.
a Vending route that includes over a dozen machines and thousands of dollars in monthly income.

persona_rationale: A rags-to-riches dollar narrative with no documentation is just a story. It's the standard 'I escaped the rat race' template, and unbacked numbers are easy to invent.

suggested_fix.current: Personal income/asset figures stated as narrative
suggested_fix.proposed: Either substantiate (screenshots, a route count that matches a public record, a named business) or shift the emphasis from the founder's personal wealth to documented member outcomes

finding: The four featured 'case studies' are video thumbnails with names only and no written outcome or context; the page never provides concrete, dated metrics (machines placed, revenue, time-to-first-location) for any member.

evidence.quote: four video tiles labeled with names only (Thomas Rohlader, Joe Natoli, Jason Priest, Anthony)

THOMAS ROHLADER

Owner, TR Vending

JOE NATOLI

Owner — Stryker300 Vending

persona_rationale: A 'case study' to me means numbers and a before/after, not a face and a play button. Without dated, specific outcomes I can't judge whether these people made money or just appeared in a video.

suggested_fix.current: Video tiles + name/company only, no written metrics
suggested_fix.proposed: Give each case study a short structured summary: starting point, time elapsed, machines/locations, and a verifiable outcome figure beneath the video

finding: The case studies page has a heading-order (skipped heading levels) violation.

evidence.quote: /case-studies … heading-order | moderate | Ensure the order of headings is semantically correct

persona_rationale: Minor on its own, but it adds to the pile of small build-quality slips that collectively tell me this site wasn't carefully finished.

suggested_fix.proposed: Reorder headings so levels do not skip (h1 → h2 → h3 in document order)

finding: Every testimonial across the home and case-studies pages is a uniform '5 out of 5 stars' with no mixed or critical feedback, which reads as curated rather than authentic.

evidence.quote: 5 out of 5 stars

persona_rationale: A perfect-score wall with zero nuance is a tell. Real customer bases have a spread; an unbroken row of 5-stars looks hand-picked, which makes me discount all of it.

suggested_fix.proposed: Either drop the star ratings (the quotes stand alone) or surface aggregate/third-party reviews (e.g. a Trustpilot or Skool community rating) that include the real distribution

finding: Submitting the application form with empty or invalid data produces no visible validation errors at all.

evidence.quote: form#1 … empty: no visible validation errors after empty submit
invalid: no visible validation errors on invalid data — entered values preserved

persona_rationale: A form that doesn't react to a bad submit makes me doubt it works at all. If I can't trust the form to validate, I won't trust it to actually deliver my application to a human.

suggested_fix.current: No inline validation on empty/invalid submit
suggested_fix.proposed: Show field-level error messages on submit ('Email is required', 'Enter a valid email') and prevent submission until required fields are valid

finding: After a successful application the only confirmation is a small green line of text beside the button; the form stays full, nothing is disabled, and there is no redirect to the existing /thank-you-for-applying page.

evidence.quote: Thanks. We received your details and will follow up shortly.
The page does not redirect to the existing /thank-you-for-applying page, does not clear or disable the form, and shows no prominent confirmation.

persona_rationale: I just gave a company I'm unsure about my name, email, phone and budget. A whisper-quiet green line isn't reassurance — I'd assume it failed and either give up or resubmit, exactly as the automation did five times.

suggested_fix.current: Inline green text 'Thanks. We received your details…', form left intact, no redirect
suggested_fix.proposed: Redirect to the existing /thank-you-for-applying page (or render a full-width success panel) that states what happens next and the expected response time

finding: The apply form collects name, email, phone, location and budget tier but shows no privacy/consent statement or link to the Privacy Policy at the point of submission.

evidence.quote:
What are you trying to build?
SUBMIT APPLICATION

persona_rationale: I read the fine print before I hand over contact details and my budget. No 'by submitting you agree to our privacy policy' line at the form means I have to leave the page to find out what happens to my data — friction at the exact moment trust matters most.

suggested_fix.current: Submit button with no consent/privacy text
suggested_fix.proposed: Add a short consent line beneath the submit button: 'By applying you agree to our Privacy Policy. We never sell your data.' with Privacy Policy linked

finding: A news card image fails with a 500 error from a leftover Webflow CDN URL (rendering raw alt text), and the page heading 'BLOG' contradicts the 'NEWS' navigation label and /news route.

evidence.quote: Failed to load resource: the server responded with a status of 500 (Internal Server Error)
500 /_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcdn.prod.website-files.com%2F…ChatGPT%2520Image…
third card shows broken image with raw alt text 'Top 10 Profitable Products to Stock…'

persona_rationale: A broken image pulling from an old Webflow CDN plus a heading that doesn't match the menu tells me this is a half-migrated site held together with tape. That undercuts every confident claim made elsewhere.

suggested_fix.current: Broken image from cdn.prod.website-files.com (500); heading 'BLOG' vs nav 'NEWS'
suggested_fix.proposed: Re-host the article images on the app's own storage/CDN and fix the 500; rename the page heading to 'NEWS' (or rename the nav item) so the section has one consistent name
